The **Aleutian Islands** ([/əˈluːʃən/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Aleutian_Islands.oga "File:En-us-Aleutian Islands.oga") [*ə-LOO-shən*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Алеутские острова, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *Aleutskiye ostrova*; [Aleut](/wiki/Aleut_language "Aleut language"): *Unangam Tanangin*, "land of the [Aleuts](/wiki/Aleut "Aleut")"; possibly from the [Chukchi](/wiki/Chukchi_language "Chukchi language") *aliat*, or "island")—also called the **Aleut Islands**,[[4]](#cite_note-4) **Aleutic Islands**,[[5]](#cite_note-5) or, before the [Alaska Purchase](/wiki/Alaska_Purchase "Alaska Purchase") in 1867, the **Catherine Archipelago**—are a chain of 14 main, larger [volcanic islands](/wiki/Volcanic_island "Volcanic island") and 55 smaller ones.

Most of the islands belong to the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), with the archipelago encompassing the [Aleutians West Census Area](/wiki/Aleutians_West_Census_Area,_Alaska "Aleutians West Census Area, Alaska") and the [Aleutians East Borough](/wiki/Aleutians_East_Borough,_Alaska "Aleutians East Borough, Alaska"). The [Commander Islands](/wiki/Commander_Islands "Commander Islands"), located further to the west, belong to the Russian [federal subject](/wiki/Federal_subjects_of_Russia "Federal subjects of Russia") of [Kamchatka Krai](/wiki/Kamchatka_Krai "Kamchatka Krai"), of the [Russian Far East](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East").[[1]](#cite_note-EBonline-1)

The islands form part of the [Aleutian Arc](/wiki/Aleutian_Arc "Aleutian Arc") of the Northern [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), and occupy a land area of 6,821 sq mi (17,666 km2) that extends westward roughly 1,200 mi (1,900 km) from the [Alaska Peninsula](/wiki/Alaska_Peninsula "Alaska Peninsula") mainland, in the direction of the [Kamchatka Peninsula](/wiki/Kamchatka_Peninsula "Kamchatka Peninsula"); the archipelago acts as a border between the [Bering Sea](/wiki/Bering_Sea "Bering Sea") to the north and the Pacific Ocean to the south. Crossing [longitude 180°](/wiki/180th_meridian "180th meridian"), at which point east and west [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") end, the archipelago contains both the westernmost and easternmost parts of the United States, by longitude ([Amatignak Island](/wiki/Amatignak_Island "Amatignak Island") and [Semisopochnoi Island](/wiki/Semisopochnoi_Island "Semisopochnoi Island"), respectively). The westernmost U.S. island, in real terms, however, is [Attu Island](/wiki/Attu_Island "Attu Island"), west of which runs the [International Date Line](/wiki/International_Date_Line "International Date Line").

The islands, with their 57 volcanoes, form the northernmost part of the [Pacific Ring of Fire](/wiki/Ring_of_Fire "Ring of Fire"). [Physiographically](/wiki/Physical_geography "Physical geography"), they are a distinct section of the larger [Pacific Border](/wiki/Pacific_Border_province "Pacific Border province") province, which, in turn, is part of the larger [Pacific Mountain System](/wiki/Geography_of_the_United_States_Pacific_Mountain_System "Geography of the United States Pacific Mountain System") physiographic division. The islands are considered to be among the most geographically isolated areas of the Northern Pacific.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Battles and skirmishes took place during the [Aleutian Islands campaign](/wiki/Aleutian_Islands_campaign "Aleutian Islands campaign") of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The Japanese landing and [occupations of Kiska](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Kiska "Japanese occupation of Kiska") and [Attu](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Attu "Japanese occupation of Attu"), in June 1942, were one of only two invasions of the United States in North America during WWII; the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam") and [Wake Atoll](/wiki/Wake_Island "Wake Island"), [Pacific territories of the United States](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States"), were also invaded.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geology")]

Motion between the [Kula Plate](/wiki/Kula_Plate "Kula Plate") and the [North American Plate](/wiki/North_American_Plate "North American Plate") along the margin of the Bering Shelf (in the Bering Sea north of the Aleutian arc) ended in the early [Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene"). The Aleutian Basin, the ocean floor north of the Aleutian arc, is the remainder of the Kula Plate that was trapped when volcanism and [subduction](/wiki/Subduction "Subduction") jumped south to its current location at c. 56 Ma.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
The Aleutian [island arc](/wiki/Island_arc "Island arc") formed in the Early Eocene (55–50 Ma) when the subduction of the [Pacific Plate](/wiki/Pacific_Plate "Pacific Plate") under the North American Plate began. The arc is made of separate blocks that have been rotated clockwise. The [basement](/wiki/Basement_(geology) "Basement (geology)") underlying the islands is made of three stratigraphic units: an Eocene layer of volcanic rock, an [Oligocene](/wiki/Oligocene "Oligocene")–[Miocene](/wiki/Miocene "Miocene") layer of marine sedimentary rock, and a [Pliocene](/wiki/Pliocene "Pliocene")–[Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary") layer of sedimentary and igneous rock.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/UnalaskaAlaska.jpg/250px-UnalaskaAlaska.jpg)](/wiki/File:UnalaskaAlaska.jpg)

[Unalaska Island](/wiki/Unalaska_Island "Unalaska Island") in the Aleutian Islands

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/North-Pacific-air-routes.png/250px-North-Pacific-air-routes.png)](/wiki/File:North-Pacific-air-routes.png)

Active Aleutian volcanoes

The islands, known before 1867 as the Catherine Archipelago, include six groups (east to west)

* [Fox Islands](/wiki/Fox_Islands_(Alaska) "Fox Islands (Alaska)") (the main islands are [Unimak](/wiki/Unimak_Island "Unimak Island"), [Akutan](/wiki/Akutan_Island "Akutan Island"), [Unalaska](/wiki/Unalaska_Island "Unalaska Island") and [Umnak](/wiki/Umnak_Island "Umnak Island"))
* [Islands of Four Mountains](/wiki/Islands_of_Four_Mountains "Islands of Four Mountains") (the main islands are [Yunaska](/wiki/Yunaska_Island "Yunaska Island") and [Chuginadak](/wiki/Chuginadak_Island "Chuginadak Island"))
* [Andreanof Islands](/wiki/Andreanof_Islands "Andreanof Islands") (the main islands are [Adak](/wiki/Adak_Island "Adak Island"), [Atka](/wiki/Atka_Island "Atka Island"), [Amlia](/wiki/Amlia_Island "Amlia Island"), [Seguam](/wiki/Seguam_Island "Seguam Island"), [Kanaga](/wiki/Kanaga_Island "Kanaga Island") and [Tanaga](/wiki/Tanaga_Island "Tanaga Island"))
* [Rat Islands](/wiki/Rat_Islands "Rat Islands") (the main islands are [Kiska](/wiki/Kiska_Island "Kiska Island") and [Amchitka](/wiki/Amchitka_Island "Amchitka Island"))
* [Near Islands](/wiki/Near_Islands "Near Islands") (the main islands are [Attu Island](/wiki/Attu_Island "Attu Island"), [Agattu Island](/wiki/Agattu_Island "Agattu Island") and the [Semichi Islands](/wiki/Semichi_Islands "Semichi Islands") - [Alaid](/wiki/Alaid_Island_(Alaska) "Alaid Island (Alaska)"), [Nizki](/wiki/Nizki_Island "Nizki Island") and [Shemya](/wiki/Shemya_Island "Shemya Island"))
* [Commander Islands](/wiki/Commander_Islands "Commander Islands") (the main islands are [Bering](/wiki/Bering_Island "Bering Island") and [Medny](/wiki/Medny_Island "Medny Island"))

All six are located between 51° and 55° N latitude and 172° E and 163° W longitude.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The largest islands in the Aleutians are [Attu](/wiki/Attu_Island "Attu Island"), and [Unalaska](/wiki/Unalaska_Island "Unalaska Island"), [Umnak](/wiki/Umnak "Umnak"), and [Unimak](/wiki/Unimak_Island "Unimak Island") in the Fox Islands. The largest of those is Unimak Island, with an area of 1,571.41 mi2 (4,069.9 km2), followed by Unalaska Island, the only other Aleutian Island with an area over 1,000 square miles (2,600 km2).

The axis of the archipelago near the mainland of Alaska has a southwest trend, but at Tanaga Island (about 178° W) its direction changes to the northwest. This change of direction corresponds to a curve in the line of [volcanic](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") fissures that have contributed their products to the building of the islands. Such curved chains are repeated about the Pacific Ocean in the [Kuril Islands](/wiki/Kuril_Islands "Kuril Islands"), the [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") chain, and in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). All these island arcs are at the edge of the [Pacific Plate](/wiki/Pacific_Plate "Pacific Plate") and experience much [seismic](/wiki/Seismology "Seismology") activity, but are still habitable; the Aleutians lie between the Pacific and North American [tectonic plates](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The general elevation is greatest in the eastern islands and least in the western. The island chain is a western continuation of the [Aleutian Range](/wiki/Aleutian_Range "Aleutian Range") on the mainland.[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11)

The great majority of the islands bear evident marks of volcanic origin, and there are numerous volcanic cones on the north side of the chain, some of them active; many of the islands, however, are not wholly volcanic, but contain crystalline or sedimentary rocks, and also amber and beds of [lignite](/wiki/Lignite "Lignite"). The coasts are rocky and surf-worn, and the approaches are exceedingly dangerous, the land rising immediately from the coasts to steep, bold mountains.[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11)

These volcanic islands reach heights of 6,200 feet (1,900 m). [Makushin Volcano](/wiki/Makushin_Volcano "Makushin Volcano") (5,691 feet (1,735 m)) on Unalaska Island, is not quite visible from within the town of [Unalaska](/wiki/Unalaska,_Alaska "Unalaska, Alaska"), though the steam rising from its cone is visible on a (rare) clear day. Residents of Unalaska need only to climb one of the smaller hills in the area, such as [Pyramid Peak](/wiki/Pyramid_Peak_(Unalaska_Island) "Pyramid Peak (Unalaska Island)") or Mt. Newhall, to get a good look at the snow-covered cone. The volcanic [Bogoslof](/wiki/Bogoslof_Island "Bogoslof Island") and [Fire Islands](/wiki/Fire_Island_(Aleutian_Islands) "Fire Island (Aleutian Islands)"), which rose from the sea in 1796 and 1883 respectively, lie about 30 miles (50 km) west of [Unalaska Bay](/wiki/Unalaska_Bay "Unalaska Bay").[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11)

In 1906, a new volcanic cone rose between the islets of Bogoslof and Grewingk, near Unalaska, followed by another in 1907. These cones were nearly demolished by an explosive eruption on September 1, 1907.[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11) In 2017, the volcanic cone erupted sending ash and ice particles 30,000 feet (9000 m) into the air.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[Alfred Russel Wallace](/wiki/Alfred_Russel_Wallace "Alfred Russel Wallace")'s 1879 book *Australasia*, Ian Todd's 1974 book *Island Realm: A Pacific Panorama* and Dean Kohlhoff's 2002 book *Amchitka and the Bomb: Nuclear Testing in Alaska* all associate the Aleutian Islands with the [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") region due to their status as remote islands in the Pacific.[[13]](#cite_note-realm-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) The islands, having biogeographical and ethnocultural affinities to [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), are not ordinarily considered a part of the region.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-handbook-17)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Kuluk_Bay%2C_Adak%2C_Alaska_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Kuluk_Bay%2C_Adak%2C_Alaska_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kuluk_Bay,_Adak,_Alaska_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Adak Island](/wiki/Adak_Island "Adak Island")'s climate creates a [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra").

The climate of the islands is oceanic, with moderate and fairly uniform temperatures and heavy rainfall. Fogs are almost constant. Summer weather is much cooler than Southeast Alaska (around [Sitka](/wiki/Sitka,_Alaska "Sitka, Alaska")), but the winter temperature of the islands and of the [Alaska Panhandle](/wiki/Alaska_Panhandle "Alaska Panhandle") is nearly the same.[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11) According to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") system, the area southwest of [53°30′N 167°00′W﻿ / ﻿53.5°N 167.0°W﻿ / 53.5; -167.0](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Aleutian_Islands&params=53.5_N_167_W_scale:10000000), on [Unalaska Island](/wiki/Unalaska_Island "Unalaska Island"), has a "Subpolar Oceanic Climate" (type "Cfc", as does [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík"), [Tórshavn](/wiki/T%C3%B3rshavn "Tórshavn"), [Punta Arenas](/wiki/Punta_Arenas "Punta Arenas"), [Ushuaia](/wiki/Ushuaia "Ushuaia") and the [Auckland Islands](/wiki/Auckland_Islands "Auckland Islands")), characterized by the coldest month averaging above 0 °C (32 °F), one to three months averaging above 10 °C (50 °F), and no significant precipitation differences between seasons. To the northeast of that point, the climate becomes "Subarctic With Cool Summers And Year Around Rainfall" (type "Dfc", like [Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky](/wiki/Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky "Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky"), [Murmansk](/wiki/Murmansk "Murmansk"), [St. Moritz](/wiki/St._Moritz "St. Moritz"), and [Labrador City](/wiki/Labrador_City "Labrador City")), where it is similar albeit colder, with the coldest month averaging below 0 °C (32 °F).[[18]](#cite_note-18) During the winter, the islands become the center of a semi-permanent [low-pressure area](/wiki/Low-pressure_area "Low-pressure area") called the [Aleutian Low](/wiki/Aleutian_Low "Aleutian Low").[[19]](#cite_note-RodinovBondOverland-19)

The mean annual temperature for Unalaska, the most populated island of the group, is about 38 °[F](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit") (3 °[C](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius")), being about 30 °F (−1 °C) in January and about 52 °F (11 °C) in August. The highest and lowest temperatures recorded on the islands were 78 °F (26 °C) and 5 °F (−15 °C), respectively. The average amount of annual rainfall is about 80 inches (2,000 mm); Unalaska, with about 250 rainy days per year, is said to be one of the rainiest places within the U.S.[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11)

## Flora

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Flora")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Cape_Promontory_Cape_Lutkes%2C_Alaska._Cape_Lutkes_is_part_of_the_Aleutian_Islands.JPG/250px-Cape_Promontory_Cape_Lutkes%2C_Alaska._Cape_Lutkes_is_part_of_the_Aleutian_Islands.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cape_Promontory_Cape_Lutkes,_Alaska._Cape_Lutkes_is_part_of_the_Aleutian_Islands.JPG)

Cape Promontory, Cape Lutkes on [Unimak Island](/wiki/Unimak_Island "Unimak Island") in the Aleutian Islands, Alaska

The growing season lasts approximately 135 days, from early in May until late in September, but agriculture is limited to the raising of few vegetables. With the exception of some stunted [willows](/wiki/Willow "Willow"), the vast majority of the chain is devoid of native trees.[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11) On some of the islands, such as [Adak](/wiki/Adak_Island "Adak Island") and [Amaknak](/wiki/Amaknak_Island "Amaknak Island"), there are a few [coniferous](/wiki/Pinophyta "Pinophyta") trees growing, remnants of the [Russian period](#Russian_period). While tall trees grow in many cold climates, Aleutian conifers — some estimated to be two hundred years old — rarely reach a height of even 10 feet (3 m), and many of them are still less than 5 feet (1.5 m) tall. This is because the islands, much like the [Falklands](/wiki/Falkland_Islands "Falkland Islands") and other islands of similar [latitudes](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), experience such strong winds that taller trees are vulnerable to snapping off.

Instead of trees, the islands are covered with a luxuriant, dense growth of herbage and shrubs, including [crowberry](/wiki/Crowberry "Crowberry"), [bluejoint](/wiki/Bluejoint "Bluejoint"), [grasses](/wiki/Poaceae "Poaceae"), [sedges](/wiki/Cyperaceae "Cyperaceae"), and many flowering plants.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-20) There are areas of peat bog near the coasts. Endemic plants include the endangered [Aleutian shield fern](/wiki/Polystichum_aleuticum "Polystichum aleuticum").

There is currently almost no naturally occurring forest in the Aleutian Islands, except with the help of human intervention. For example, the “Adak Forest” on [Adak Island](/wiki/Adak_Island "Adak Island") is a man-made tree group consisting of 33 trees.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Fauna")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Killerwhales_jumping.jpg/250px-Killerwhales_jumping.jpg)](/wiki/File:Killerwhales_jumping.jpg)

Transient [orcas](/wiki/Orca "Orca") near Unimak Island, eastern Aleutian Islands

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Amak_Island%2C_Steller%27s_Sea_Lion_haul_out.jpg/250px-Amak_Island%2C_Steller%27s_Sea_Lion_haul_out.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amak_Island,_Steller%27s_Sea_Lion_haul_out.jpg)

[Steller sea lions](/wiki/Steller_sea_lion "Steller sea lion") haul out on [Amak Island](/wiki/Amak_Island "Amak Island").

The Aleutians are home to many large colonies of seabirds. [Buldir Island](/wiki/Buldir_Island_(Alaska) "Buldir Island (Alaska)") has 21 breeding seabird species, including the Bering Sea-endemic [red-legged kittiwake](/wiki/Red-legged_kittiwake "Red-legged kittiwake"). Large seabird colonies are also present at [Kiska](/wiki/Kiska "Kiska"), [Gareloi](/wiki/Gareloi_Island "Gareloi Island"), [Semisopochnoi](/wiki/Semisopochnoi_Island "Semisopochnoi Island"), [Bogoslof](/wiki/Bogoslof_Island "Bogoslof Island"), and others. The islands are also frequented by vagrant Asiatic birds, including the [common rosefinch](/wiki/Common_rosefinch "Common rosefinch"), [Siberian rubythroat](/wiki/Siberian_rubythroat "Siberian rubythroat"), [bluethroat](/wiki/Bluethroat "Bluethroat"), [lanceolated warbler](/wiki/Lanceolated_warbler "Lanceolated warbler"), and the first North American record of the [intermediate egret](/wiki/Intermediate_egret "Intermediate egret").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The habitats of the Aleutians are largely unspoiled, but wildlife is affected by competition from introduced species such as cattle, [caribou](/wiki/Caribou "Caribou"), and [foxes](/wiki/Fox "Fox"). Nearly all of the Aleutians are protected as part of the [Alaska Maritime National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Alaska_Maritime_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Alaska Maritime National Wildlife Refuge") and the [Aleutian Islands Wilderness](/wiki/Aleutian_Islands_Wilderness "Aleutian Islands Wilderness").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Observations have identified [sea otters](/wiki/Sea_otter "Sea otter") as a [keystone species](/wiki/Keystone_species "Keystone species") along the coasts of many of the Aleutian Islands. Their presence encourages the growth of [kelp forests](/wiki/Kelp_forests "Kelp forests"), as the otters control [sea urchin](/wiki/Sea_urchin "Sea urchin") populations (as large populations of sea urchins can create [urchin barrens](/wiki/Urchin_barrens "Urchin barrens") by clearing away kelp stands).[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Residences_in_Adak._Adak_Island%2C_Aleutian_Islands%2C_Alaska_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Residences_in_Adak._Adak_Island%2C_Aleutian_Islands%2C_Alaska_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Residences_in_Adak._Adak_Island,_Aleutian_Islands,_Alaska_-_panoramio.jpg)

Residences in [Adak](/wiki/Adak,_Alaska "Adak, Alaska"), Adak Island

The native people refer to themselves as Unangan, and are now generally known by most non-natives as the [Aleut](/wiki/Aleut_people "Aleut people"). The [Aleut language](/wiki/Aleut_language "Aleut language") is one of the two main branches of the [Eskimo–Aleut](/wiki/Eskimo%E2%80%93Aleut_languages "Eskimo–Aleut languages") language family. This family is not known to be related to any others. The [2020 U.S. census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") recorded a population of 7,152 on the islands, of whom 4,254 were living in the main settlement of [Unalaska](/wiki/Unalaska,_Alaska "Unalaska, Alaska").

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Dutch_harbor_crab_boats.jpg/250px-Dutch_harbor_crab_boats.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dutch_harbor_crab_boats.jpg)

Dutch harbor [crab boats](/wiki/Alaskan_king_crab_fishing "Alaskan king crab fishing")

On the less mountainous islands, the raising of [sheep](/wiki/Domestic_sheep "Domestic sheep") and [reindeer](/wiki/Reindeer "Reindeer") was once believed to be practicable.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-20) There are [bison](/wiki/Bison "Bison") on islands near Sand Point. Sheep raising seems to have died off with the advent of synthetic fibers, which lowered the value of wool. During the 1980s, there were some [llama](/wiki/Llama "Llama") being raised on Unalaska. The current economy is primarily based on [fishing](/wiki/Fishing_industry "Fishing industry"), and the presence of U.S. military. The only crop is [potato](/wiki/Potato "Potato"). [Chickens](/wiki/Chicken "Chicken") are raised in barns under protection from the cold.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transportation")]

In addition to a partial air service and a ferry service, the [Alaska Marine Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Marine_Highway "Alaska Marine Highway") passes through many of the U.S. islands.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: History")]

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

Because the islands stretch like a broken bridge from the continent Asia to the continent North America, many anthropologists[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] hypothesize they were a route of the [first human occupants of the Americas](/wiki/Settlement_of_the_Americas "Settlement of the Americas"). However, the earliest known evidence in public [recorded history](/wiki/Recorded_history "Recorded history") of human occupation in the Americas is found much farther south. The early human sites in the Bering Sea were probably submerged by rising waters and sand during the current [interglacial](/wiki/Interglacial "Interglacial") period.

People living in or on the Aleutian Islands developed skills in hunting, fishing, and basketry. The baskets were woven with shredded stalks of beach rye.

### Russian period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Russian period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Aleut.jpg/250px-Aleut.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aleut.jpg)

An [Aleutian](/wiki/Aleut "Aleut") man with a [Creole](/wiki/Alaskan_Creole_people "Alaskan Creole people") woman in the Aleutian Islands

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Das_Innere_einer_Winterwohnung_auf_Unalaschka.jpg/250px-Das_Innere_einer_Winterwohnung_auf_Unalaschka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Das_Innere_einer_Winterwohnung_auf_Unalaschka.jpg)

A Creole winter home in [Unalaska](/wiki/Unalaska "Unalaska"), Aleutian Islands

Explorers, traders and missionaries arrived from Russia beginning in 1741.

In 1741 the [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") government sent [Vitus Bering](/wiki/Vitus_Bering "Vitus Bering"), a Danish-born Russian, and [Aleksei Chirikov](/wiki/Aleksei_Chirikov "Aleksei Chirikov"), a Russian, in the ships *Saint Peter* and *Saint Paul* on a voyage of discovery in the Northern Pacific. After the ships were separated by a storm; Chirikov discovered several eastern islands of the Aleutian group, and Bering discovered several of the western islands. Bering was shipwrecked and died in the Komandorski Islands ([Commander Islands](/wiki/Commander_Islands "Commander Islands")); one of which now bears his name ([Bering Island](/wiki/Bering_Island "Bering Island")), along with the surrounding Bering Sea. The survivors of Bering's party reached the [Kamchatka Peninsula](/wiki/Kamchatka_Peninsula "Kamchatka Peninsula") in a boat constructed from the wreckage of their ship, and reported the islands were rich in fur-bearing animals.[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11)

Siberian fur hunters flocked to the Commander Islands and gradually moved eastward across the Aleutian Islands to North America. In this manner, Russia gained a foothold on the northwestern coast of North America. The Aleutian Islands consequently belonged to Russia, until that country transferred all its possessions in North America to the U.S. in the 1867 [Alaska Purchase](/wiki/Alaska_Purchase "Alaska Purchase").[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11)

In the 1780s Russian merchant and seafarer [Grigory Shelikhov](/wiki/Grigory_Shelikhov "Grigory Shelikhov") established a [company](/wiki/Shelikhov-Golikov_Company "Shelikhov-Golikov Company") based on the systematic exploitation of the indigenous peoples, with whom there was sporadic conflict with the native population (frequently disastrous to the poorly armed and vastly outnumbered Russians), for example the [Awa'uq Massacre](/wiki/Awa%27uq_Massacre "Awa'uq Massacre"). The colonies soon entered a relatively stable state based on cooperation, intermarriage, and official policies that provided social status, education, and professional training to children of mixed Aleut-Russian birth.[[26]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-26) Within a generation, the day-to-day administration of the Russian-American colonies was largely in the hands of native-born Alaskans. Reversing the usual trend in colonization where indigenous technologies are replaced, the Russians adopted the Aleut kayak, or [baidarka](/wiki/Baidarka "Baidarka"), sea otter hunting techniques, and the working of native copper deposits. The Russians instituted public education, preservation of the Aleut language through transliteration of religious and other texts into Aleut via an adaptation of the Cyrillic alphabet, vaccination of the native population against [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), and science-based sea mammal conservation policies that were ahead of their time.[[26]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Unalaska_church.jpg/250px-Unalaska_church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Unalaska_church.jpg)

Russian Orthodox Church on Unalaska Island

By 1760 the Russian merchant Andrian Tolstykh had made a detailed census in the vicinity of [Adak](/wiki/Adak_Island "Adak Island") and extended Russian citizenship to the Aleuts.

During his third and last voyage in 1778, Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") surveyed the eastern portion of the Aleutian archipelago, accurately determined the position of some of the more important islands, and corrected many errors of former navigators.[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11)

#### Orthodox Christian heritage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Orthodox Christian heritage")]

Among the first [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") missionaries to arrive in the Aleutian Islands was a party of ten [Russian Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") monks and priests, who arrived in 1793. Within two years, a monk named Herman was the only survivor of that party. He settled on [Spruce Island](/wiki/Spruce_Island_(Alaska) "Spruce Island (Alaska)"), near [Kodiak Island](/wiki/Kodiak_Island "Kodiak Island"), and often defended the rights of the Aleuts against the Russian trading companies. He is now known in the Orthodox Church as Saint [Herman of Alaska](/wiki/Herman_of_Alaska "Herman of Alaska").

Another early Christian missionary of the Russian Orthodox Church was Father Veniaminov who arrived in [Unalaska](/wiki/Unalaska_Island "Unalaska Island") in 1824. He was named Bishop Innokentii in 1840 and moved to [Sitka](/wiki/Sitka,_Alaska "Sitka, Alaska"). He is now known in the Orthodox Church as Saint [Innocent of Alaska](/wiki/Innocent_of_Alaska "Innocent of Alaska").

The principal settlements were on Unalaska Island. The oldest was Iliuliuk (also called Unalaska), settled in 1760–1775, with a customs house and an Orthodox church.

### U.S. possession

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: U.S. possession")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6e/AleutianIslands.jpeg)](/wiki/File:AleutianIslands.jpeg)  
Western Aleutian Islands, from a 1916 map of the Alaska Territory

After the American [purchase of Alaska](/wiki/Alaska_Purchase "Alaska Purchase") from Russia in 1867, further development took place. New buildings included a [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") mission and orphanage, and the headquarters for a considerable fleet of United States [revenue cutters](/wiki/United_States_Revenue_Cutter_Service "United States Revenue Cutter Service"), which patrolled the [sealing](/wiki/Seal_hunting "Seal hunting") grounds of the [Pribilof Islands](/wiki/Pribilof_Islands "Pribilof Islands").[[11]](#cite_note-EB1911-11) The first public school in Unalaska opened in 1883.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Attu_village_1937.jpg/250px-Attu_village_1937.jpg)](/wiki/File:Attu_village_1937.jpg)

Attu village, [Attu Island](/wiki/Attu_Island "Attu Island") in June 1937

The [U.S. Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") extended American citizenship to all Native Americans in 1924; this law has been held to include the indigenous peoples of Alaska. A hospital was built in Unalaska in 1933 by the U.S. [Bureau of Indian Affairs](/wiki/Bureau_of_Indian_Affairs "Bureau of Indian Affairs").

#### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: World War II")]

Main article: [Aleutian Islands campaign](/wiki/Aleutian_Islands_campaign "Aleutian Islands campaign")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Attu_landing_craft_on_beach_1943.jpg/250px-Attu_landing_craft_on_beach_1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:Attu_landing_craft_on_beach_1943.jpg)

U.S. soldiers of the southern landing force on the beach at [Massacre Bay](/wiki/Massacre_Bay_(Alaska) "Massacre Bay (Alaska)"), Attu Island during the [Battle of Attu](/wiki/Battle_of_Attu "Battle of Attu"), May 11, 1943

On June 3, 1942, which was during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), in what were the only two invasions of the United States during the war, small parts of the Aleutian islands were occupied by [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") forces, when [Attu](/wiki/Attu_Island "Attu Island") and [Kiska](/wiki/Kiska "Kiska") were invaded possibly to divert American forces away from the main Japanese attack at [Midway Atoll](/wiki/Midway_Atoll "Midway Atoll").[[28]](#cite_note-28) The U.S. Navy, having broken the [Japanese naval codes](/wiki/Japanese_naval_codes "Japanese naval codes"), proceeded as if this was just a diversion,[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) and it did not expend large amounts of effort in defending the islands. More than 90 Americans were taken to Japan as prisoners of war.

The United States moved most of the remaining civilian population (over 800) of the Aleutians and Pribilovians to camps in the [Alaska Panhandle](/wiki/Alaska_Panhandle "Alaska Panhandle").

In May 1943, American forces invaded Japanese-held Attu and defeated the Japanese. In August 1943, American and Canadian troops launched an invasion of [Kiska](/wiki/Kiska "Kiska"), in which 34,426 men composed of both Americans and Canadian participated; however, Japanese forces had already evacuated the island, ending the campaign in the islands. The invasion was an embarrassment for the Allied forces as the entire Japanese force of 5,183 men had left the island on July 28 without the Americans noticing; however, the Americans suffered significant casualties during their "invasion"—313 men died as a result of accidents, with many dying due to accidental fire.

President Roosevelt visited Adak in 1944, meeting with commanders and eating with soldiers of the garrison. This was his first and only trip to the Aleutian Islands and Alaska as a whole. A rumor spread that FDR had accidentally left his Scottish Terrier "Fala" on one of the Islands and had to send a destroyer to retrieve the dog, costing taxpayers several million dollars. The President made fun of these rumors during a talk with the Teamsters Union in Washington DC, now known as the "Fala Speech". At this speech the President joked with the crowd saying, "Well, of course, I don't resent attacks, and my family doesn't resent attacks, but Fala does resent them!"[[32]](#cite_note-32)

June 3, 2002, was celebrated as Dutch Harbor Remembrance Day. The governor of Alaska ordered state flags lowered to half-staff to honor the 43 Americans who died during the two-day Japanese air attack in 1942. The [Aleutian World War II National Historic Area](/wiki/Aleutian_World_War_II_National_Historic_Area "Aleutian World War II National Historic Area") Visitors Center opened that month.

### Statehood era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aleutian_Islands&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Statehood era")]

The U.S. conducted [underground tests](/wiki/Underground_nuclear_testing "Underground nuclear testing") of [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon") on [Amchitka Island](/wiki/Amchitka "Amchitka") from 1965 to 1971 as part of the [Vela Uniform](/wiki/Vela_Uniform "Vela Uniform") program. The final detonation, the [Cannikin](/wiki/Cannikin "Cannikin"), was the largest underground [nuclear explosion](/wiki/Nuclear_explosion "Nuclear explosion") by the U.S.

The [Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act](/wiki/Alaska_Native_Claims_Settlement_Act "Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act") became law in 1971. In 1977, the Ounalashka Corporation (from Unalaska) declared a [dividend](/wiki/Dividend "Dividend"). This was the first village corporation to declare and pay a dividend to its shareholders.

The Aleutian Islands were designated a [UNESCO biosphere reserve](/wiki/UNESCO_biosphere_reserve "UNESCO biosphere reserve") in 1976. The Aleutians were one of 17 biosphere reserves in the United States withdrawn by request of the U.S. government from the programme in June 2017.[[33]](#cite_note-UNESCO-33)[[34]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Press_Release-34)
